summary: Jeff Atwood highlights two examples of poor UI design that violate established conventions. The first example shows an installer displaying elapsed time to the tenth of a second instead of the standard estimated remaining time, rendering it useless for users trying to plan their waiting time. The second example criticizes a MySQL error page that mimics Windows app conventions instead of following web standards, while also exposing technical jargon like 'threads' and 'OS-dependent bugs' that mean nothing to average users. The post argues that conventions exist for good reasons and breaking them without purpose only harms user experience.
key_points: Installer progress indicators should show estimated time remaining, not elapsed time—the latter tells users nothing useful
Showing elapsed time to the tenth of a second compounds the uselessness with absurd precision
Web apps should follow web conventions, not try to mimic Windows applications
Error messages should be meaningful to the actual users who will see them, not expose internal technical details
money_quotes: Occasionally I run into UI elements so boneheaded, I have to wonder what the programmers were thinking.
Not only elapsed time, but elapsed time down to the tenth of a second.
Way to take a useful installer convention, reverse it, and utterly ruin it. Bravo!
Web apps should not pretend to be windows apps; they should follow standard web conventions.
I doubt 'threads' or 'OS-dependent bugs' mean anything to most users browsing xbitlabs articles.
key_insight: UI conventions exist because they serve users, and violating them without understanding why they exist creates experiences that are worse than useless.
